tool_args.label: Bus Rapid Transit Phase II: Public Input
tool_args.raw_text: Bus Rapid Transit Phase II: Public Input report published June 2010 by NYC DOT and MTA NYCT documenting public workshops and online survey for BRT Phase II.

payload.title: Bus Rapid Transit Phase II: Public Input
payload.publisher: New York City Department of Transportation (DOT) and MTA New York City Transit (NYCT)
payload.description: Report documenting public workshops and online survey results for BRT Phase II expansion in New York City

evidence_refs.source_quote: June 2010
New York City Department of Transportation (DOT) and MTA New York City Transit (NYCT) began exploring opportunities for the expansion of Bus Rapid Transit (BRT) in New York City
TABLE OF CONTENTS

payload.description: Planning effort by DOT and NYCT to develop a comprehensive BRT system across New York City, including identifying unmet transit needs, public workshops, and online survey.
payload.lead_agency: New York City Department of Transportation (DOT)

evidence_refs.source_quote: In the fall of 2008, the New York City Department of Transportation (DOT) and MTA New York City Transit (NYCT) began exploring opportunities for the expansion of Bus Rapid Transit (BRT) in New York City.

In April of 2009, the New York City Department of Transportation (DOT) and MTA New York City Transit (NYCT) released 'Introduction to Bus Rapid Transit Phase II,' a report on the future of Bus Rapid Transit (BRT) in New York City.

payload.description: Seven public workshops held across the five boroughs to educate the public about BRT, solicit feedback on unmet transit needs, and solicit feedback on BRT features
organizers: New York City Department of Transportation (DOT)

evidence_refs.source_quote: DOT and NYCT held seven public workshops between May 28, 2009 and June 18, 2009 on the future of BRT in New York City

warnings: event.organizers is relation context, not a closed enum or identity key. Organizer lists refer to entity records. Suggested relation: organized_by.

payload.description: Online survey for the BRT Phase II project, implemented using SurveyMonkey, similar in structure and content to the city-wide BRT Phase II workshops
organizers: New York City Department of Transportation (DOT)

evidence_refs.source_quote: From June to July 2009, the New York City Department of Transportation (DOT) and MTA New York City Transit (NYCT) conducted an online survey for the BRT Phase II project.

warnings: event.organizers is relation context, not a closed enum or identity key. Organizer lists refer to entity records. Suggested relation: organized_by.

tool_args.label: Upper West Side/Upper East Side Crosstown Corridor

payload.corridor_name: Upper West Side/Upper East Side Crosstown Corridor

86th Street received the greatest attention; participants stated that the M86 is very slow and over-crowded

payload.description: High priority corridor; ranked first by all Queens survey respondents; would provide improved airport access for Queens residents

payload.description: Underserved area difficult to get to without a car; most common suggestion was for a BRT route between Red Hook and Downtown Brooklyn

evidence_refs.source_quote: Red Hook
Four out of five participant tables at the Downtown Brooklyn workshop identified Red Hook as an underserved area

payload.description: Participants interested in stimulating economic development; Richmond Terrace and Snug Harbor identified as growth areas for development and tourism

evidence_refs.source_quote: North Shore Corridor
A number of participants were interested in stimulating economic development in this area with a BRT route

payload.description: High priority for BRT, both for employees who worked in the area and residents; suggestions included converting the Bx6 route to BRT

evidence_refs.source_quote: Hunts Point Peninsula

tool_args.label: Strong public support for BRT expansion

payload.claim_text: The public is overwhelmingly supportive of BRT expansion and other bus improvements in New York City
payload.description: 98% of workshop attendees and 97% of survey respondents expressed support for BRT expansion
payload.subject: public sentiment on BRT

evidence_refs.source_quote: 98% of workshop attendees and 97% of survey respondents expressed support for BRT expansion

tool_args.label: Frequent service most desirable speed feature

payload.claim_text: Frequent service was viewed as the most desirable BRT feature among workshop participants in the speed and reliability category
payload.description: Of the features designed to improve bus speed and reliability, frequent service was the most popular
payload.subject: BRT features preference

evidence_refs.source_quote: frequent service was viewed as the most desirable feature among participants

tool_args.label: Real-time info most popular comfort feature

payload.claim_text: Real-time bus arrival information was by far the most popular comfort and convenience feature among workshop participants
payload.description: Of the features designed to improve comfort and convenience, real-time bus arrival information was the most popular
payload.subject: BRT features preference

evidence_refs.source_quote: real-time bus arrival information was by far the most popular feature

tool_args.label: No clear favorite bus lane treatment

payload.claim_text: No bus lane or busway treatment was the clear favorite among workshop participants, although curbside lanes were the least popular treatment
payload.description: Participants had varied preferences across bus lane configurations (curbside, offset, busway)
payload.subject: bus lane design preference

evidence_refs.source_quote: No treatment was the clear favorite among participants, although curbside lanes were the least popular treatment
